smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: made these for you
thread.body_text:
hi Ellie, spent a few minutes on the Twisted Pepper site and I'm a little annoyed we're not working together yet haha.

but that's not why I reached out. I handle Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of sending a BS pitch, I built you some.

built 3 ad concepts from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and customer reviews. it's all done and waiting on my side.

should I send it over?

- Kyle

thread.subject: RE: made these for you
thread.body_text:
Ellie - the thing most brands notice first isn't the ads, it's the brand playbook. it maps your voice, the exact words your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's ready to go with the mockups.

 want me to pass both along?

- Kyle
please send me the brand playbook please

here you go Ellie - 3 ad mockups built from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus your brand playbook .

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Twisted Pepper is a real gourmet dip/soup/rub brand with 103 products and a strong founder story, but no ads running and almost no reviews on site, so this is a build-from-zero play starting with their low-price hero mixes bundled to clear the $40 free-shipping bar.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A handcrafted gourmet food brand founded by Ellie Hughes in 2005. They make dip mixes, soup mixes, BBQ rubs and seasonings. They sell direct to consumers online and also do wholesale and fundraisers.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: 103 products from $5.50 to $48.99. Median price is $9.99, which I am using as the stand-in for their average order value since we have no access to their real numbers. Single mixes are cheap ($6 to $10). They also sell 3-packs and 4-packs ($22.99 to $36) and gift boxes, which is how they push order size up.
deep_dive.positioning: Approachable gourmet. Fine-restaurant flavor made fast and easy for busy home cooks. Gluten free, low sodium, no MSG. Not mass-market bland.
deep_dive.who_buys: Busy home cooks who want bold flavor without the work, plus gift-givers buying multi-packs and gift boxes, especially around holidays.
customer_voice: I am absolutely addicted to your soups and dips/cheese ball mixes. To be honest - all those I bought from you never went for gifts! They went in our stomachs, and to share with extended family and friends over the holidays.
Jalapeno Lime Rub is absolutely amazing. I use in avacado toast, deviled eggs, steak and chicken.
My husband Loves the Pig Sweat Rub. He sprinkles on his bacon while cooking, and anything pork! I order 4 jars at time.
The Enchilada Sauce Seasoning is so authentic, I don't have to make my Grandmother's Recipe.
strengths: Strong founder story and a clear voice. Ellie is a real person with 20 years behind the brand, which makes great ad material.
Built-in path to bigger orders. The 4-packs and gift boxes let us push people past the $40 free-shipping line, which fixes the low single-item price problem.
gaps: Almost no visible social proof. A few products have 1 to 7 reviews, most have none. That makes cold ads harder to convert.
They run no Meta ads that we can find, so there is no history to build on. We are starting from scratch, and the shipping policy page even says 'Coming Soon,' which is a trust gap for new buyers.

game_plan.headline: Start from the Scroll-Stopper ads, prove one bundle can sell cold, then scale the winner and add a gift angle.

phases.goal: Prove that cold traffic will buy at all. Find one ad and one product that work before we spend real money.
campaigns: One simple sales campaign testing the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads against cold audiences
A basic retargeting campaign for anyone who visits the site but does not buy
lead_products: Lead with the 4 Pack Dipping Madness or 4 Pack Veggies & Chiles Dip Mixes at $22.99. Reason: the dips are their signature line (Ellie started with dips, and the homepage leads with them), and the 4-pack lands close to the $40 free-shipping bar so it is easy to nudge people over with a second item. A single $6 dip mix cannot carry ad costs on its own.
Also test the Jalapeno Lime Rub as a standalone hero, because it has the strongest unprompted customer quote we have ('absolutely amazing... I use in avocado toast, deviled eggs, steak and chicken').
phases.creative: Go live first with the Scroll-Stopper ad that leans on the bold-flavor, made-at-home angle, since that matches their best review language. Whichever of the 3 sheet ads speaks to the dip 4-pack goes live day one. We pair each ad with the free-shipping-over-$40 offer.
phases.budget: Start around $50 a day. Keep it small until one ad shows it can sell.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We can see which ad gets the cheapest clicks and whether any of them lead to a sale. We are not chasing profit yet. We are buying information.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad and pause the losers
Test 2 to 3 new versions of the winner (new hooks, new images)
Widen the audience beyond the tightest interests
lead_products: Keep pushing the winning bundle from month one. If the Jalapeno Lime Rub won as a standalone, build a small rub bundle around it so orders clear $40.
phases.creative: Build fresh ads that copy the angle of whatever worked. Add short founder-voice creative featuring Ellie, since the story is a real asset and cold buyers trust a face.
phases.budget: Step up toward $100 to $150 a day, but only on the parts that are already working.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad holds up at higher spend and starts returning more than it costs. We have 2 or 3 ads that reliably sell, not just one.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and add a second product line so we are not resting on one item.
campaigns: Launch a second product angle (gift boxes or soup 3-packs)
Keep retargeting running and refresh it with new creative
lead_products: Add the Comfort Soup Collection 3 pack or Home Cookin' Soup Collection 3 pack ($30) as a gift and cold-weather angle heading into fall. Reason: soups are half their catalog, sit at a healthy $30 price, and match the gifting behavior we already see in reviews ('a huge hit... over the holidays').
phases.creative: Introduce a clear gift-focused ad set, since day 90 lands close to the holiday gifting season and their own reviews say people buy these as gifts.
phases.budget: Scale in steps as long as the return holds. Never jump budget faster than the results allow.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two product lines each selling from ads, spend climbing without the return falling apart, and a base of retargeting and email signups we can lean on for the holidays.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: Enough to get roughly 25 to 40 clicks a day at typical food/ecom click costs, which is the floor for learning which ad and product work before we spend more.

estimates.label: Average order value we planned around
estimates.value: About $10 per order as a starting stand-in
estimates.basis: Their median product price is $9.99. We used that because we have no access to their real order data. This is exactly why we push 4-packs and the $40 free-shipping bar to lift real order size.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We are not putting a dollar figure on this before the call
estimates.basis: We cannot see their real order value, margin, or conversion rate. A revenue promise built on a $10 guess would be dishonest. Once Ellie tells us her real average order value and margin, we can set a target return and a real number.

assumptions: Their real average order value is higher than $10 because of 4-packs and gift boxes. If most orders are single $6 mixes, paid ads will be very hard and the plan changes.
Ellie can ship fast and reliably, since the site promises fast shipping but the shipping policy page still says 'Coming Soon.'
There is enough margin on a $22.99 4-pack to survive ad costs. We need her to confirm this.
The 3 Scroll-Stopper ads are approved and ready to run as-is.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, or have you ever? On Meta or anywhere else?
questions.listening_for: We found no Meta ads. Confirm whether they never started or stopped. If they stopped, ask why, that tells us what already failed.

questions.question: Who handles marketing today, you or someone on your team?
questions.listening_for: Is Ellie doing it all herself? That affects how hands-on we need to be and whether she can approve creative fast.

questions.question: What is your actual average order value, and does one order usually hold one item or a few?
questions.listening_for: This is the make-or-break number. Median price is $10. If real orders are $35+ because of bundles, we are in good shape. If they are $6, paid ads may not work.

questions.question: Roughly what does it cost you to make and ship a $22.99 4-pack? I am not asking for exact, just is there room to pay for an ad and still make money.
questions.listening_for: Is there enough margin to survive ad costs. Thin margin on cheap food means we must stick to bundles and gift boxes only.

questions.question: When someone new lands on your site, what usually stops them from buying?
questions.listening_for: Her gut read on the leak. Watch for hints about price, trust, or shipping. It tells us if the problem is the ad or the site.

questions.question: I noticed most products have very few reviews on the site. Do you have reviews or photos from happy customers somewhere else, like email or Facebook?
questions.listening_for: Do they have social proof we can put in ads. Their best asset is customer quotes. If she has more hiding in her inbox, that changes our creative a lot.

questions.question: Your shipping policy page still says 'Coming Soon.' How fast do orders actually go out, and what do you charge for shipping under $40?
questions.listening_for: A blank shipping page kills trust for new buyers. Find out the real shipping speed and cost so we can put it in ads and fix the page.

questions.question: Which products actually sell the most for you right now, online or at events?
questions.listening_for: Confirm or correct our bestseller guess. We assumed dips and the Jalapeno Lime Rub. If her real bestseller is different, we lead with that instead.

questions.question: How much of your business is gifts versus people buying for themselves?
questions.listening_for: Reviews suggest heavy gifting. If true, the month-90 gift-box push moves up and holiday timing becomes central.

questions.question: Do you get sales from people searching Google for things like 'gluten free dip mix' or a specific rub, or is it mostly people who already know you?
questions.listening_for: People clearly search for seasonings, dips, and rubs by name. That is a real opening for Google Search ads later. If she says most sales are repeat or word of mouth, Google can capture the searches she is missing.

questions.question: You mention wholesale and fundraisers. How big is that side, and do you want more of it?
questions.listening_for: If wholesale matters, that is a different marketing job. Note it, but keep the call focused on Meta first. Do not overpromise.

objections.objection: My products are cheap. How can ads make money on a $6 dip mix?
objections.answer: You are right, we would never advertise a single $6 mix. We lead with your 4-packs at $22.99 and use your own free-shipping-over-$40 offer to nudge people to add a second item. That is how we get an order big enough to pay for the ad and still leave you a profit.

objections.objection: I have never run ads and I do not want to burn money learning.
objections.answer: That is exactly why month one starts small, around $50 a day. We are not chasing profit in the first 30 days, we are buying information about which of your products sells cold. We only spend more once one ad proves it works.

objections.objection: I do not really have reviews or fancy photos to advertise with.
objections.answer: You have better than that. Your customers already say things like 'absolutely addicted' and 'the Jalapeno Lime Rub is amazing.' Those real words become the ads. And part of the early work is collecting more reviews from your buyers so the ads keep getting stronger.

objections.objection: I am one person and I am already busy. I cannot manage this.
objections.answer: You do not manage it, we do. What we need from you is quick approvals, your real order and margin numbers, and any customer quotes or photos you already have. We build and run everything else.

red_flags.flag: Average order value may be too low to carry paid ads.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Median price is $9.99 and many items are $6. If real orders are single cheap items, ad costs will eat any profit. The whole plan depends on bundles lifting order size, so confirm this before promising anything.

red_flags.flag: Almost no reviews visible on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold buyers need proof from other buyers. With few or no reviews on product pages, ads have to work harder and conversion will be lower until we add social proof.
A blank shipping page is a trust killer at checkout for new customers who do not know the brand. This needs fixing before or right as ads go live.
We start from zero, with no past data on what audiences or creative work. Expect a real learning phase in month one, and do not promise fast profit.

red_flags.flag: Food and health claims are tightly limited.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We must not say low sodium helps blood pressure, gluten free treats any condition, or no MSG is healthier. Kyle should not let the call drift into health promises. Sell on flavor and ease, not health outcomes.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads later. People actively search for things like 'gluten free dip mix,' 'BBQ rub,' and specific seasonings by name, so there is real demand to capture once Meta is proven.

SEO on their recipe pages. They already publish recipes tied to their products (Spinach Artichoke Dip, Lentil Soup, Classic Pea Soup). These can pull in free search traffic over time and feed product sales, so it is an honest fit, not a forced one.
